card.indications: Treatment of selected susceptible bacterial infections, including complicated urinary, intra-abdominal, bone and joint, skin, lower-respiratory, anthrax, and plague infections. Ophthalmic and otic products treat susceptible local infections. Reserve systemic use for appropriate indications because of serious adverse effects.
card.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication, transcription, repair, and recombination. It is bactericidal.
card.dosing: Systemic dosing depends on infection site, severity, organism, route, and renal function. Adult oral regimens commonly range from 250 to 750 mg every 12 hours; IV regimens commonly use 400 mg every 8 to 12 hours. Renal adjustment is often required.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses with water and separate from antacids, iron, zinc, magnesium, calcium supplements, sucralfate, and similar products—commonly at least 2 hours before or 6 hours after. Do not take with dairy products or calcium-fortified juice alone. Shake suspension well. Infuse IV over the labeled time.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to ciprofloxacin or other quinolones and concurrent tizanidine use. Avoid in patients with myasthenia gravis when possible. Use caution with seizure disorders, QT prolongation, aneurysm risk, severe renal impairment, and prior serious quinolone reactions.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include tendon inflammation or rupture, peripheral neuropathy, disabling CNS effects, and exacerbation of myasthenia gravis. Additional risks include aortic aneurysm or dissection, QT prolongation, dysglycemia, seizures, psychiatric effects, hepatotoxicity, C. difficile diarrhea, and photosensitivity.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, headache, dizziness, insomnia, rash, and local irritation with eye or ear products.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Tendon rupture, irreversible neuropathy, seizures, severe psychiatric effects, aortic rupture, serious dysrhythmia, severe hypoglycemia, hepatotoxicity, anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, and C. difficile colitis.
card.interactions: Tizanidine is contraindicated. Cations reduce oral absorption. Ciprofloxacin strongly inhibits CYP1A2 and can increase theophylline, caffeine, clozapine, and other substrates. Warfarin effect may increase. QT-prolonging drugs and corticosteroids increase specific risks.
card.monitoring: Monitor cultures, infection response, renal function, glucose in at-risk patients, neurologic symptoms, tendon pain, bowel pattern, liver tests when indicated, ECG in high-risk patients, and interactions such as INR or theophylline levels.
card.patientTeaching: Stop the drug and contact a clinician for tendon pain, numbness, tingling, weakness, severe anxiety, confusion, fainting, or severe diarrhea. Avoid excessive sun, maintain hydration, separate minerals and antacids, and complete the prescribed course unless told to stop for a serious reaction.
card.nutrition: Take with water. Food is allowed, but avoid taking the dose with dairy or calcium-fortified juice alone. Separate mineral supplements as directed. Limit excessive caffeine because ciprofloxacin can increase caffeine effects.

card.indications: Part of combination chemotherapy for advanced testicular, ovarian, bladder, head and neck, lung, and other cancers according to regimen-specific protocols.
card.mechanism: Forms highly reactive platinum complexes that cross-link DNA, inhibit DNA replication and transcription, and trigger apoptosis. Cytotoxicity is not limited to a specific phase of the cell cycle.
card.dosing: Dose and schedule are regimen specific and based on body-surface area, cancer type, kidney function, and prior toxicity. Common protocols use either a single dose per cycle or daily doses for several days. Dose reductions or delay may be required for renal, marrow, neurologic, or hearing toxicity.
card.routeAdministration: Administer only by trained oncology personnel. Provide vigorous prehydration and posthydration and electrolyte replacement as ordered. Use compatible tubing and avoid aluminum-containing needles or sets because aluminum can react with cisplatin. Give prescribed antiemetics before treatment.
card.contraindications: Severe hypersensitivity to cisplatin or other platinum compounds, significant renal impairment, marked myelosuppression, or hearing impairment when risks outweigh benefits. Avoid use in pregnancy unless specifically justified.
card.warnings: Major hazards include severe cumulative nephrotoxicity, profound nausea and vomiting, myelosuppression, ototoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, electrolyte wasting, and anaphylaxis. Dosing errors can be fatal, especially if cisplatin is confused with carboplatin.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Severe nausea and vomiting, fatigue, anorexia, metallic taste, alopecia, anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and electrolyte losses including magnesium and potassium.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute kidney injury, irreversible hearing loss, severe peripheral neuropathy, febrile neutropenia, sepsis, anaphylaxis, seizures, thromboembolism, severe electrolyte abnormalities, and secondary malignancy.
card.interactions: Other nephrotoxic or ototoxic drugs such as aminoglycosides, amphotericin B, loop diuretics, and contrast agents increase toxicity. Myelosuppressive agents increase infection and bleeding risk. Some anticonvulsant levels may decrease during therapy.
card.monitoring: Before each cycle monitor serum creatinine, creatinine clearance, BUN, magnesium, potassium, calcium, CBC, liver tests, hydration status, urine output, hearing, and peripheral neurologic function. Monitor for infection, bleeding, and severe emesis.
card.patientTeaching: Report reduced urine, swelling, ringing in the ears, hearing change, numbness, fever, bleeding, or uncontrolled vomiting immediately. Follow hydration instructions and infection precautions. Use reliable contraception and keep all laboratory and hearing-test appointments.
card.nutrition: Aggressive hydration and electrolyte replacement are central to therapy. Use small frequent meals and prescribed antiemetics for nausea. Maintain calories and protein; oncology nutrition support may be needed for persistent taste changes or weight loss.
